gh-f1.answer:
Your first Java program. Every Java application starts with a class and a main method — the JVM looks for public static void main(String[] args) and runs from there.

Requirements:
1. Class name must be HelloWorld (matches the filename HelloWorld.java).
2. Use System.out.println to print at least one line of text.
3. Add a second println with your name or a short message about Week 1.

Key concepts: class declaration, main method signature, println vs print (println adds a newline), semicolons at the end of statements, and curly braces for blocks.
gh-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: Every Java app — from Android to Spring Boot — starts with main(). Recruiters often check if your GitHub has a clean HelloWorld as proof you can set up JDK + IDE.

Compile & run (terminal in your project folder):
javac HelloWorld.java
java HelloWorld

Expected: two lines printed, no errors.

Push to java-week-1:
git add HelloWorld.java
git commit -m "Add HelloWorld.java — first Java program"
git push

Tip: If javac says 'class HelloWorld is public, should be declared in a file named HelloWorld.java', your filename and class name don't match — fix that first.

gh-f2.answer:
Demonstrate variables — named boxes that hold values while your program runs.

Requirements:
1. Declare at least one variable of each type: int, double, char, boolean, and String.
2. Assign meaningful values (not just x, y, z — use names like age, height, gradeLetter).
3. Print each variable with a label so output is readable.
4. Change one variable's value after the first print (e.g., age = 11) and print again to show reassignment.

Key concepts: declaration (type name = value), camelCase naming, String is a reference type (use double quotes), char uses single quotes ('A'), and + concatenates text with numbers.
gh-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: Games store health and score in variables; banking apps store balance. Readable names (studentCount vs n) are what interviewers look for in code reviews.

Compile & run:
javac Variables.java
java Variables

Push:
git add Variables.java
git commit -m "Add Variables.java — primitives and Strings"
git push

Common mistake: int age = "10" — types must match. String age = "10" is correct for text.

gh-f3.answer:
Show all eight Java primitive types and their typical use.

Requirements:
1. Declare one variable for each primitive: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean.
2. Print each with its type name and value.
3. Demonstrate one widening conversion (int assigned to double automatically).
4. Demonstrate one explicit cast (double truncated to int with (int)).

Primitive cheat sheet:
• byte / short / int / long — whole numbers (pick int for most counts)
• float / double — decimals (pick double for measurements and money)
• char — single character
• boolean — true or false only

String is NOT a primitive — it's a class — but you'll use it constantly alongside primitives.
gh-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: Using int for money loses paise; using double for loop counters is unnecessary. Real apps pick the smallest correct type for memory and clarity.

Compile & run:
javac DataTypes.java
java DataTypes

Push:
git add DataTypes.java
git commit -m "Add DataTypes.java — all primitives and casting"
git push

Watch out: int a = 5, b = 2; double c = a / b; gives 2.0 not 2.5 — at least one operand must be double (a / 2.0).

gh-f4.answer:
Practice Java operators — symbols that perform calculations and comparisons.

Requirements:
1. Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, % on two int variables. Print each result.
2. Relational: compare two numbers with ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=. Print boolean results.
3. Logical: combine two boolean expressions with && and ||.
4. Increment: use ++ on a counter and print before/after.
5. Optional: ternary operator — String result = (score >= 40) ? "Pass" : "Fail";

Operator precedence (highest first): parentheses → * / % → + - → comparisons → && → ||. When unsure, add parentheses.

Integer division: 7 / 2 is 3, not 3.5. Modulus % gives remainder: 7 % 2 is 1.
gh-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: Billing systems multiply quantity * price; login checks combine isActive && !isLocked. Operators are in every line of production Java.

Compile & run:
javac Operators.java
java Operators

Push:
git add Operators.java
git commit -m "Add Operators.java — arithmetic and logic"
git push

gh-f5.answer:
Learn Scanner — the standard way to read keyboard input in beginner Java.

Requirements:
1. import java.util.Scanner;
2. Create Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
3. Prompt the user, then read: a String (name), an int (age), and a double (height or salary).
4. Print a summary sentence using all three values.
5. Call scanner.close() at the end (good habit even in small programs).

Methods you'll use:
• nextLine() — full line of text (best for names)
• nextInt() — integer
• nextDouble() — decimal

Tip: After nextInt(), call nextLine() once to consume the leftover newline before reading a full sentence — a very common beginner bug.
gh-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: Console input becomes form fields and API requests later. Scanner teaches the pattern: prompt → read → validate → use.

Compile & run:
javac ScannerDemo.java
java ScannerDemo

Type sample input when prompted (e.g., Riya, 16, 1.55).

Push:
git add ScannerDemo.java
git commit -m "Add ScannerDemo.java — keyboard input"
git push

gh-f6.answer:
Build a simple two-number calculator — combines variables, operators, and Scanner.

Requirements:
1. Read two numbers from the user (double is safest so division works).
2. Read an operator character: +, -, *, or /.
3. Use if-else (or switch) to perform the correct operation.
4. Print the result clearly: "Result: 12.5"
5. Handle division by zero — print an error message instead of crashing.

This is your first program with decision-making (if-else). Each branch runs only when its condition is true.
gh-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: Every app that computes totals — cart checkout, tip calculator, unit converter — uses the same read → decide → calculate → display flow.

Compile & run:
javac Calculator.java
java Calculator

Test with: 10, 4, + and also 10, 0, / to verify your zero check.

Push:
git add Calculator.java
git commit -m "Add Calculator.java — if-else and Scanner"
git push

gh-f7.answer:
Student Information System — a mini console app that collects and displays student details. This mirrors your Week 1 project.

Requirements:
1. Use Scanner to read: full name (String), roll number (int), age (int), branch (String), and percentage (double).
2. Print a formatted summary — either labeled lines or printf.
3. Use meaningful variable names (studentName, rollNumber, not a, b, c).
4. Optional bonus: print "Pass" or "Fail" if percentage >= 40.

printf format hints:
• %s — String
• %d — int
• %.2f — double with 2 decimal places
• \n — newline
gh-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: CRUD apps start as "read input, store in variables, show on screen." Your GitHub README plus this file shows you can build a small complete feature — exactly what trainers and hiring managers want in Week 1 portfolios.

Compile & run:
javac StudentInfo.java
java StudentInfo

Push:
git add StudentInfo.java
git commit -m "Add StudentInfo.java — console student form"
git push

gh-f8.answer:
BMI (Body Mass Index) calculator — applies formulas and conditional logic.

Formula: BMI = weight (kg) / (height (m) × height (m))

Requirements:
1. Read weight in kg and height in meters using Scanner.
2. Compute BMI and print it rounded to 2 decimal places (printf %.2f).
3. Print a category using if-else if:
   • BMI < 18.5 → Underweight
   • 18.5 to 24.9 → Normal
   • 25 to 29.9 → Overweight
   • 30+ → Obese
4. Reject invalid input: weight or height <= 0.

Categories follow standard WHO ranges — use them exactly so your output matches expected test cases.
gh-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: Health apps, fitness trackers, and insurance tools all transform raw inputs into a computed metric plus a label. Same pattern: validate → calculate → classify → display.

Compile & run:
javac BMI.java
java BMI

Try weight 70, height 1.75 — BMI ≈ 22.86, category Normal.

Push:
git add BMI.java
git commit -m "Add BMI.java — formula and if-else categories"
git push

gh-f9.answer:
Grade calculator — convert a numeric score into a letter grade using if-else if chains.

Typical scale (use this unless your instructor says otherwise):
• 90–100 → A
• 80–89 → B
• 70–79 → C
• 60–69 → D
• Below 60 → F

Requirements:
1. Read marks (int or double) from Scanner.
2. Validate: 0–100 only; otherwise print an error.
3. Print both the numeric score and letter grade.
4. Optional: also print "Pass" if marks >= 40, else "Fail".

Check conditions from highest to lowest (>= 90 first) so the first matching branch is correct.
gh-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: Grading, tax slabs, shipping tiers, and discount rules are all "given a number, pick a bucket." Mastering if-else if here prepares you for switch statements and later enum-based logic in enterprise Java.

Compile & run:
javac GradeCalculator.java
java GradeCalculator

Test edge cases: 90 (A), 89 (B), 59 (F), 101 (error).

Push:
git add GradeCalculator.java
git commit -m "Add GradeCalculator.java — score to letter grade"
git push

gh-f10.answer:
README.md is the front page of your repository — anyone (including interviewers) sees it first on GitHub.

Write a README that includes:
1. Title: # java-week-1
2. Short description — what this repo contains (Week 1 Java basics programs).
3. Topics covered — bullet list: variables, data types, operators, Scanner, if-else, etc.
4. How to run — JDK version you used, compile command (javac *.java), run example (java HelloWorld).
5. File list — one line per .java file and what it does.
6. What you learned — 3–5 sentences in your own words (not copied from notes).
7. Optional: your name, course, and date.

Use Markdown: # headings, - bullets, `inline code` for commands, and ```java blocks if you show a snippet.
gh-f10.realWorld:
Why it matters: A repo without a README looks unfinished. A clear README proves you can communicate — a skill as important as coding. Many hiring managers never clone your code; they only read the README and skim one file.

No compile step for README — preview in your IDE or on GitHub after push.

Push:
git add README.md
git commit -m "Add README — Week 1 summary and run instructions"
git push

After pushing, open your repo URL in the browser and confirm the README renders with headings and code formatting.
gh-f10.code:
# java-week-1

Week 1 Java fundamentals — console programs for Prathyu Academy.

## Topics covered
- Hello World and program structure
- Variables and primitive data types
- Operators and expressions
- Scanner for user input
- if-else decisions (calculator, BMI, grades)

## How to run
Requires JDK 17+.

```bash
javac *.java
java HelloWorld
```

## Files
| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| HelloWorld.java | First program, println |
| Variables.java | Primitive and String variables |
| ... | (complete the table for all 9 programs) |

## What I learned
This week I learned how Java programs start from main(), how to store data in typed variables, and how to read user input with Scanner. I also practiced if-else for decisions like grade calculation.
